body_txt: When characters consume food or intangible services at a business that they are expected to pay money for afterwards. However, they don't have the funds to pay when the bill — usually a Shockingly Expensive one — arrives. The management will agree to let them work off their debt as an alternative to involving the authorities. A common setting for this trope are restaurants, where the characters will be required to wash up the dishes. Even though most modern restaurants have machines to do that, the unfortunate victims will invariably have to wash by hand note It should be noted that some restaurants have washing machines that only sanitize the dishes, and any residue will need to be washed off manually before they can be put in . Usually unexplained is how the restaurant was going to get the dishes clean if no deadbeats showed up that night. Rarely played straight these days , as employment standards regulations in most jurisdictions disallow the payment of workers in anything other than legal currency, and workers' compensation legistation prevent workers from delivering services without being on the payroll and insured in case of injury. That said, these kinds of arrangements are considered under the table. There's also the issue of the general public being untrained when it comes to washing and sanitizing dishes in a commercial kitchen. No good manager is going to let an unknown person who can't pay go in there without first verifying whether or not they are fit for duty, and that's not something that can be decided in an instant. The restaurant would risk a future customer getting food poisoning from an improperly sanitized dish or the deadbeat getting injured on a wet floor or sharp object, both of which might lead to lawsuits. Related to Trapped by Gambling Debts . Indentured Servitude is where this is formalized and legally enforced. Dine and Dash is an attempt at defying this trope.
